responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Congenital Versus Environmental: An Unconscious Meaning11

Burness Moore M.D.

Burness Moore

Burness Moore  

It is a common observation in psychiatric practice that patients will insist that their problems are a congenital affliction for which there is no real hope of cure. Perhaps closely allied is the stubborn belief among some doctors, even including psychiatrists, that all psychiatric disorders must have an organic basis. The content of an analytic session revealed one unconscious meaning as the basis for such a belief. It represents a complex intellectualized defense which may be expressed briefly thus: "I am not to blame for my incestuous masturbation fantasies and acts. They are not my fault, but mother's, since I was born with a penis which causes me to behave this way. Therefore, I do not need to fear losing my penis, but may continue to enjoy myself." Although it is hazardous to generalize, it may be that the unconscious basis for the belief in this case may apply to other instances as well.

A middle-aged man sought treatment because he had fallen in love with a prostitute (2). Despite his conscious intentions, he had been unable to free himself of his attachment during two years of analysis. On several occasions he expressed the wish that he might be hypnotized and directed to return to his wife. One day he mentioned having seen a television program about a marital reconciliation court in which the marital partners were bound by contract to reconcile their differences and could be held in contempt of court for a breach of contract. He expressed the wish that he could be bound by such a contract to be faithful to his wife.

Submitted March 2, 1959

1Presented at the Midwinter Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, New York, December, 1958.312

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Some Ethological Studies of Social Relationships and Conflict Situations11

I. Kaufman M.D.

I. Kaufman

I. Kaufman  

SUMMARY

The paper presents data from animal-behavior studies, including original work by the author, which deal with the development of social bonds. "Imprinting" is an important early learned basis of attachment behavior but only in certain species and perhaps not in man.

The anaclitic role of feeding for the development of sexuality and object relations is seen as an important but not exclusive determinant. Comfort in contact and clinging is another important basis of early attachment to an object. Such findings indicate the value of Lorenz's concept of the parent companion as the object, a personal knowledge of whom is built up by virtue of the fact that the eliciting and terminating stimuli for various discrete responses are there united. Bowlby has hypothesized that this mechanism is the basis of the human child's tie to his mother. His hypothesis requires further empirical confirmation. Since the parent-companion relationship is experientially determined, it seems unlikely that there is a primary-object-relations drive.

The author's experiments on chicks indicate the early ontogenetic development of a social bond through social experience. Further, it appears that disruption of the bond by separation produces a state of distress. The relationship of the separation distress to anxiety and depression are speculated upon. Further studies of separation reactions in animals might help clarify the relationship between separation, anxiety, and depression.

Ethological methods of behavioral analysis are described. Behavioral tendencies are inferred from specific action patterns, the causes and functions of which are studied. In social situations of animals it is found that conflicting tendencies are motivated. Among these conflicting tendencies three are especially prevalent—sexuality, aggression, and fleeing. It is suggested that the same is true also of the human, certain of whose behaviors might be better understood if the tendency to flee was more adequately appreciated as being also basic.

The phenomenology of animals in conflict is categorically described, indicating the similarities to human reaction patterns. The

684

genetic significance of these responses to conflict, as well as of responses to deprivation, is to be found in the fact that such responses may become fixated and continue to appear when the external situation no longer seems to warrant such behavior. This too is compared to human patterns of behavior in health and disease and is considered worthy of further study by psychoanalysts.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Percept as a Screen: Economic and Structural Aspects11

Norman Reider M.D.

Norman Reider

Norman Reider  

I

The problem of screen memories is an old one, largely treated in the past from an economic and dynamic point of view. Freud (7) first touched on these considerations when he wrote of "some people whose earliest recollections of childhood are concerned with everyday and indifferent events … recollected (too clearly, one is inclined to say) in every detail. …" In the same paper Freud explains: "It is a case of displacement on to something associated by contiguity; or, looking at the process as a whole, a case of repression accompanied by replacement by something in the neighbourhood (whether in space or time)."

In the first quotation above I would emphasize both too and clearly, an emphasis which Freud already sensed as a problem in itself. Something recollected "too clearly" he later described as the "vividness" of the screen, for example, in his attempts to recall the name of the artist, Signorelli (8):

… as long as the name of the painter remained inaccessible to me, I had more than a clear visual memory of the cycle of his frescoes, and of the picture of himself in the corner; at least it was more intensive than any of my other visual memory traces. In another case, also reported in my essay of 1898, I had hopelessly forgotten the street name and address connected with a disagreeable visit in a strange city, but—as if to mock me—the house number appeared especially vivid,

Submitted November 28, 1958

1Read at the meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, December 6, 1958. Previously read at meetings of the Topeka, San Francisco, and Seattle Psychoanalytic Societies. Reference will be made to some of the discussion at these meetings.82

[[RunningHead]]

On the Question of Primary Object Need—Ethological and Psychoanalytic Considerations11

Hilda Rollman-Branch M.D.

Hilda Rollman-Branch

Hilda Rollman-Branch  

SUMMARY

Ethological studies of attachment behavior indicate an instinctive need for companionship in animals which is independent of feeding. If the critical period to form attachment to an object passes unused, a fear and flight (avoidance) reaction sets in. Infants need a certain (not as yet quantified) amount of human contact aside from the satisfaction of their physical needs. Deficiency in this contact with mother or mother substitute around the age of six months may lead to marasmus and death. The infant's capacity to elicit and utilize maternal care plays an important role. Similarities and differences between animal attachment behavior and human object relationships are discussed.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Teaching of Psychoanalytic Technique11

Rudolf Ekstein Ph.D.

Rudolf Ekstein

Rudolf Ekstein  

Edith Jacobson, while favoring full discussion of all problems concerning the teaching of psychoanalytic technique, felt that time limitations may not permit us to go much beyond certain primary issues which are concerned with the fundamentals of what should and what can be taught. The questions as to the how of teaching, as important as they are, may therefore not get as full a hearing as is ultimately desirable.

Rudolf Ekstein, in his opening paper on "A Historical Survey on the Teaching of Psychoanalytic Technique, " traced the main trends in the development of our teaching techniques as they are reflected in psychoanalytic literature. He divided the sixty or more years of psychoanalytic history into approximately two equal parts. The last thirty years are the phase of organized training, while the first phase, which he considered as an ahistorical one as far as the study of training methods is concerned, is characterized as a preinstitutionalized phase in which the personal informal arrangements between master and apprentice constituted the then-existing training patterns. Control analysis and training analysis were not separated and one may assume that psychoanalytic technique was learned through Lehranalyse, a mixture of both, and usually a short experience in the very early days of training after the turn of the century.

One anecdote, told by Bernfeld in an unpublished paper, was quoted as a useful sample of what was the prevailing spirit up to the early 20's when the first institute was organized in Berlin. Bernfeld states:

In 1922 I discussed with Freud my plan to establish myself as a practicing psychoanalyst in Vienna. I had heard that our group in Berlin encouraged psychoanalysts, especially beginners, to have a didactic analysis before they started psychoanalyzing. I asked Freud whether he did not think I ought to have an analysis first. His answer was: "Nonsense. You go right ahead. You'll get into trouble. Then we will figure out what to do about it.

responseSet.title: On Scierneuropsia—A Previously Unnamed Psychogenic Visual Disturbance
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On Scierneuropsia—A Previously Unnamed Psychogenic Visual Disturbance11

Peter Martin M.D.

Peter Martin

Peter Martin  

SUMMARY

This study is based on three patients whose chief complaint was difficulty in seeing, accompanied by a sensation of seeing objects as if through a screen. This symptom occurred in patients who might be called ambulatory schizophrenics or borderline characters or belong in the category which Freud designated as the narcissistic neuroses. The symptom is a result of a narcissistic emotional disturbance in object relationships, leading regressively to a libidinal reinvestment in secondary narcissism. This results in an infantile fixation. The physiological dysfunction in the eyes has resulted from pathological emotional investment in a visual hallucination, a waking screen. This screen expresses a wish to relax into sleep in order to prevent a disruption of destructive impulses or a psychosis from becoming overt. It also sympbolizes the cold hostile separation from the world.

I have observed otherwise "normal" individuals who had transitory episodes of clouding of vision, particularly following acute infectious diseases. When referred to ophthalmologists they are reassured that nothing is wrong with them and the symptoms disappear. Ophthalmologists are often aware of benign transitory

80

neurotic reactions when a patient complains, "It sometimes gets black in front of my eyes." Occasionally this may be the forerunner of more serious reactions. Some "normal" individuals report similar transitory sensations occurring occasionally. They say this is particularly so in the morning before they "get going."

Scierneuropsia, then, is an example of a psychogenic visual symptom, which has its roots in one of the fantasies of the earliest infant-mother relationships. It is found as a transitory sensation in "normal" individuals. Its occurrence in everyday life after awakening from sleep is not uncommon. When it remains, the symptom often follows a dream which continues in waking life in the form of the symptom. This symptom has been described and explained in psychoanalytic literature, but has never been named. For the purpose of convenience in referring to the symptom, I propose the name scierneuropsia.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Relationship Between Therapy of Children and Superego Development

Irving Kaufman M.D.

Irving Kaufman

Irving Kaufman  

Observations relevant to superego phenomena can be made in relation to:

1. Longitudinal studies of personality development. Studies of this sort are being conducted at several centers throughout the country. We are still in the earlier phases of this research.

2. The pathologic distortions of the superego demonstrated in the various emotional disturbances. This is historically the oldest research tool afforded to psychoanalysis and is certainly not yet exhausted.

3. Changes in the superego during the course of therapy. Included here are reconstruction of the developmental phases and observation of the effect of the transference-countertransference interactions on the superego within the context of the total personality.

This paper will primarily relate to the second and third areas of study of the superego as they are revealed during the course of therapy. The clinical data are drawn from both my private analytic practice and particularly from the research unit in juvenile delinquency and to some extent the research on childhood schizophrenia at the Judge Baker Guidance Center.

Our theoretical framework is based on the concepts evolved by Freud (5) who discussed the definitive development of the superego as a separate and special functional system occurring with the resolution of the oedipal conflict. He stated that developmentally the child passes through the oral and anal stages to enter the phallic-urethral stage of development with its attendant castration anxiety.

Submitted June 9, 1958

130

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Clinical Note on a Dream Followed by Elation11

Irving Harrison M.D.

Irving Harrison

Irving Harrison  

SUMMARY

A woman who had never been able to accept her lack of a penis had two dreams, during a period of life experiences which intensified her greed, her envy, and her feelings of deprivation. In the dreams, the maternal breast (penis) was represented by a dream screen equivalent: a pane of glass. These dreams were followed by a clinical elation.

This sequence of events confirms Lewin's hypothesis according to which elation may be understood as the repetition of a childhood dream. No clinical confirmation of this view has heretofore been reported.

Book Notices

PEPTIC ULCER and PSYCHOANALYSIS, by Angel Garma. Nervous and Mental Disease Monographs No. 85. Baltimore: Williams & Wilkins, 1958, vii + 143 pages, $6.00.

Angel Garma states that gastric and duodenal ulcers are dependent upon emotions following conflicts in individuals of specific personality type. According to him, the dependence of such patients "through being in love with someone who does not satisfy them genitally acts more traumatically because they are often people with a professional activity that demands too much of them and … prevents them from discharging the emotional tension of their conflicts." Mothers of the ulcer type are usually affectionate and capable but domineering and aggressive. They feed the baby well but often withhold solid food too long and encourage the child's dependence. Independence is achieved at the cost of renouncing genital satisfactions. In later conflict situations, the image of the bad internalized mother acts aggressively upon the individual's stomach. Excessive remorse, unconsciously based upon sensations of being bitten internally, is provoked. This induces alimentary disorders. Treatment should be intensive and profound and designed to modify the unconscious personality.

THEORY OF PSYCHOANALYTIC TECHNIQUE, by Karl A. Menninger. New York: Basic Books, 1958, xiii + 206 pages, $4.75.

Menninger calls attention to Fenichel's observation on the paucity of literature, not on psychoanalytic technique, but on the theory of technique. In this book he proposes to examine "some of the psychodynamic principles operative in the practice" of psychoanalysis.

[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis and Ethology11

Mortimer Ostow M.D.

Mortimer Ostow

Mortimer Ostow  

The keynote of the discussion was struck by Max Schur, in his opening remarks: Why should psychoanalysts be interested in ethology? First, he said, psychoanalysis is a general psychology. It reaches out into many disciplines and often catalyzes them. Second, both psychoanalysis and ethology are developmental psychologies—developmental in ontogenetic and phylogenetic senses. Both are concerned with studying the ontogenetic and phylogenetic evolution of behavior patterns. Schur proposed that the term "instinctual" be applied only to the concept of instinctual drive as used in psychoanalytic theory, and that, for the sake of clarity, the term "instinctive" be used to refer to the ethologic concept of instinct.

The first prepared paper, "The Study of Animal Behavior in Relation to Psychoanalysis, " was given by Charles W. Tidd. He deplored the fact that the many similarities and parallels between animal and human behavior, and the striking relation between analytic and ethologic theories have seduced many analysts into forming hasty conclusions. It is important to distinguish between mere analogy and true homology. This caution was repeated by other speakers too.

Tidd proceeded to review briefly the symposium on ethology which took place at the 1959 Congress of the International Psychoanalytic Association in Copenhagen. There were three principal speakers. John Bowlby proposed that in the human mother-child relation, the child's tie to the mother can be better understood in terms of a reciprocal social relationship founded on instinctive response systems in the infant, such as clinging and following, than by the common view that the tie is secondary to the mother's providing food and care for the child. (See J. Bowlby: The Child's Tie to his Mother. Int. J. Psychoanal., 39: 350-373, 1958.—M.O.) It was Tidd's opinion that there was not sufficient evidence to abandon the concept of orality as being of paramount importance. The second speaker in Copenhagen was I. C. Kaufman, also a participant in this panel.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Self-Rocking in Infancy11

Sylvia Brody Ph.D.

Sylvia Brody

Sylvia Brody  

SUMMARY

I have offered the hypotheses that repetitious and agitated self-rocking occur among infants with particular kinds of libidinal experiences, and that its specific aim is to re-establish body contact with the mother. These are hypotheses that can be tested by further observations. If correct, they would substantiate the suggestion of Anna Freud (9) that rocking is an autoerotic substitute

490

for satisfactions originally provided by the mother. I have also indicated that infants who rock stereotypically are likely to be hampered in the development of their object relations, and that the rocking of apparently normal infants may have serious pathological implications.

[[RunningHead]]

The American Psychoanalytic Association 1960

Bernard Bandler M.D.

Bernard Bandler

Bernard Bandler  

A presidential address is almost an anomaly in the history of the American Psychoanalytic Association. In the forty-nine years of our existence there have been nine addresses in all, one third of them delivered by William Allison White. Six of the addresses concern themselves with the affairs, the problems, and the goals of the Association; the first being White's 1928 address in which he looked forward to the development of institutes; the last being Ives Hendrick's which he delivered in this room five years ago on the "Professional Standards of the American Psychoanalytic Association." On reading these addresses, one gets a sense of tumultuous growth, of increasing strength and authority, and of the dedicated purpose which has marked this Association's stewardship of the science of psychoanalysis and of psychoanalytic training during the span of almost half a century. The hope expressed by Franz Alexander in his 1932 address on "Psychoanalysis Comes of Age, " that the expression "psychoanalytic movement" will sound as strange as "ophthalmological movement, " has been realized. To the contemporary ear, the phrase "psychoanalytic movement" has indeed a quaint and archaic sound. Whether psychoanalysis was of age is another question. Robert P. Knight, in his 1952 address on "Organized Psychoanalysis in America, " found the Association in adolescence, but with a good prognosis. Of age or not, the Association—because of its authority and its influence—is now confronted with increasing responsibilities.

We have recently completed a survey of psychoanalytic education. Our educational house in many ways is in superb order.

Submitted May 18, 1960

389

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Panel Reports—The Role of the Dream in Child Analysis11

Gaston Blom M.D.

Gaston Blom

Gaston Blom  

In her introduction, Eveoleen Rexford, referred to the 1955 panel on "The Dream in the Practice of Psychoanalysis"2 in which Editha Sterba expressed the opinion that in children the analysis of dreams played a less significant part of analytic work than that of play acting and daydreaming. However, she had noted that children whose symptomatology predominantly involved a single erotic zone (as in bed wetting, soiling, thumb sucking) reported dreams more frequently and used them in analytic work. She also stated that the child is not able to associate freely in a manner similar to the adult because his ego cannot yet differentiate between an observing and an experiencing function.

The ensuing discussion at the 1955 panel disclosed conflicting opinions about the frequency of dreams in analysis, the reporting of dreams, and their usefulness in child therapy. The contributors whose experience was essentially confined to adult cases stressed developmental aspects in children which made the use of dream material in child analysis difficult. Several child analysts stated that in their experience children do report dreams and referred to the techniques that made it possible to use them in analysis.

Rexford continued with a brief but comprehensive survey of the analytic literature on dreams in children. In the first edition of The Interpretation of Dreams, Freud stated that dreams of children were simply wish fulfillments and raised no problems for solution. In later editions and in other papers, he indicated that distorted dreams were already found in children of four or five years of age.

[[RunningHead]]

The Development of a Fetishistic Perversion—The Contribution of Preoedipal Phase Conflict

Charles Socarides M.D.

Charles Socarides

Charles Socarides  

In 1931 Freud, in his paper on "Female Sexuality" (11), clearly foresaw the difficulties and controversies inherent in the study of clinical phenomena based on unresolved conflicts of the period of life which antedates the oedipal phase. "Our insight into this early, pre-Oedipus phase … comes to us as a surprise, comparable in another field with the effect of the discovery of the Minoan-Mycenaean civilization behind that of Greece. Everything connected with this first mother-attachment has in analysis seemed to me so elusive, lost in a past so dim and shadowy, so hard to resuscitate, that it seemed as if it had undergone some specially inexorable repression" (pp. 253-254). Difficulties notwithstanding, Freud proceeded to evolve a remarkably accurate and verifiable theoretical structure of psychic events and behavior during that period for the female, touching only briefly on events occurring in the male. Subsequently, other observers have added materially to our knowledge of that period. Major contributions have been made by Ruth Mack Brunswick (4), (6), Lampl-de Groot (22), Jacobson (18), Kestenberg (19), and van der Leeuw (27). Ruth Mack Brunswick's study of these phenomena appeared three years before Freud's. He refers to her 1928 paper entitled "The Analysis of a Case of Paranoia" (5), wherein delusional jealousy in a female was traced to her preoedipal attachment to her sister.

Submitted January 26, 1959

1The word preoedipal will be used thoughout as it refers to object relations centered around the mother both in boys and girls during the period of life prior to the oedipal period. Pregenital refers to oral and anal stages of developing sexuality.281

[[RunningHead]]

On Some Uses of Psychoanalysis11

Sidney Axelrad

Sidney Axelrad

Sidney Axelrad  

One of the charges made by critics of Freud is that psychoanalytic theory sets sharp limits to the perfectibility of man, and so it does. When they criticize the biological elements of the theory, they fail to realize that these elements also guarantee man's uniqueness and individuality; for the theory also sets limits to man's ability to be manipulated and it insists that there is an irreducible, stubborn, active core of individuality demanding fulfillment and gratification, no matter what the claims of society or the demands of culture. In the early days of psychoanalysis it was felt that if children were properly reared, and if childhood sexuality could be satisfied while at the same time the trauma of infancy and childhood could be avoided, then the oedipus complex would either pass or be smashed; and if there were a sensible method of contraception and a sane sexual morality, so that the actual neuroses would vanish, then neurosis would either vanish or be so severely reduced that the rational aspects of man's psyche would prevail. To be sure, this might take three generations, but the drying up of the sources of anxiety would be under way once the tenets of psychoanalysis were widely accepted. At a minimum, the result would be the sexual liberation of the child and the adult.

To Socialists and Communists even early psychoanalytic theory was bad utopian dogma. Although not politically reactionary, it laid so little stress upon economic factors that it was useless to those movements. Economic matters were of obvious importance to the psychoanalysts; at least in Europe,

responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Psychology of Imagination
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Psychology of Imagination11

David Beres M.D.

David Beres

David Beres  

SUMMARY

1. Imagination is defined as the capacity to form a mental representation of an object, an affect, a body function, or an instinctual drive, not actually or immediately present to the senses. It

is a capacity limited to human beings and is distinguished from the capacity to form memory traces. The imaginative process is examined in terms of the structural hypothesis and the energic concepts of psychoanalysis.

2. Imagination in this study is considered as a ubiquitous psychic manifestation noted in normal mentation, pathological mental processes, and artistic creativity.

3. The imaginative process develops in the child out of earlier presymbolic mental functioning and appears with structuralization of the ego.

4. Hallucinatory wish fulfillment in the infant is a late development and presupposes the capacity to form mental representations.

5. Primary-process imagination is distinguished from secondary-process imagination according to whether the imaginative process serves immediate or delayed discharge of psychic energies and according to the role of ego control. Thought is by definition a process that binds energies and delays discharge. It is thus always a secondary-process phenomenon, though its products may be used regressively for primary-process discharge.

6. Memory traces enter into the formation of mental representations, but only in the latter can the image be evoked by a thought or a wish rather than by a direct external stimulus (which may be a stimulus from an inner organ).

7. Freud's formulation that cathexis is always directed toward mental representations (Vorstellungen) is reiterated.

8. Some clinical applications of the imaginative process are considered.

In emphasizing, as I have done, the ubiquitous nature of the imaginative process, I have sought to apply this concept to psychoanalytic theory and practice. I have attempted to indicate the different manifestations of the imaginative process. At the pinnacle of human imagination is what we call the creative imagination, but in my opinion it is not different from other imaginative functions: it adds a new factor, the synthetic function—and by this addition it creates a new product.

Imagination is a pervasive function of the human mind. In its manifold aspects it serves all components of the psychic

267

structure. It is comprised of ego functions, but it serves id drives as well as superego demands. Imagination supplies the material for symptom and defense, but it also supplies the ego with its synthetic function and its creative function. Both aspects of imagination together give to man his unique position in the animal kingdom.

responseSet.title: Some Aspects of the Role of Imagination in the Analytic Process
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Some Aspects of the Role of Imagination in the Analytic Process11

Victor Rosen M.D.

Victor Rosen

Victor Rosen  

Imagination is a word that is used rather loosely in the English language. It is, however, capable of definition and seems to describe a psychological process that we are aware of intuitively whether or not its precise boundaries have been semantically delimited. This discussion is an attempt to offer one definition of this activity and to explore the usefulness of re-examining certain aspects of psychic functioning in the light of a process so defined.

A survey of the psychopathology of imagination, which may be implied by the title of this presentation, would be a most ambitious project and would, in the end, become a clinical treatise on the organic and functional disturbances of the mental apparatus. I propose here, to discuss a limited and rather discrete area of the disturbance of this mental function as it relates to analytic resistance and the analytic process, and to suggest some alternative formulations on the dynamics of the transference as it is ordinarily encountered in the analytic procedure.

The ensuing discussion is based upon the assumption that imagination is a thought process with special characteristics. It is proposed that the prototype of the capacity for imagination in early ego development is connected with the development of "object constancy" and the behavior toward vanished objects. As in later ego development, similar mechanisms are utilized to extend the process of concept formation.

Submitted July 18, 1959

1Presented as part of a panel discussion on Imagination at the Spring Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, on April 25, 1959.229

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Panel Reports—Criteria for Analyzability11

Samuel Guttman M.D.

Samuel Guttman

Samuel Guttman  

In her introductory remarks, Elizabeth Zetzel suggested that discussion of the Criteria for Analyzability could be facilitated by differentiating between the analytic situation and the analytic process. The analytic situation might be defined as the setting in which patient and analyst share mutual identification with a common therapeutic goal. The analytic process concerns intrapsychic changes, in the patient, which are mobilized, worked through, and resolved in a successful analysis. For purposes of illustration Zetzel selected Freud's Dora, the Rat Man, and the Wolf Man, who all showed particular problems with respect to the analytic situation and the analytic process. The case of the Wolf Man, in addition, draws attention to a fact too often forgotten. "Psycho-analytic therapy, " Freud said in 1904, "was created through and for the treatment of patients permanently unfitted for life and its greatest triumph has been that a satisfactory number of these have been rendered permanently fit for existence." However, as Freud indicated in "Analysis Terminable and Interminable, " some unanalyzed residue remains following the most successful analysis—underlining the difference between the theoretically ideal and the clinically justifiable.

These patients illustrate very dissimilar responses to the analytic situation: premature flight into health or symptomatic acting out (Dora); interminable analysis (Wolf Man), and lastly intense anxiety making adherence to the formal requirements of the analytic situation impossible at that time (Rat Man). It was the Rat Man who met, to the highest degree, the criteria integral to the analytic situation: a motivation for more than symptomatic relief; a capacity to tolerate anxiety and frustration; the ability to maintain a stable relationship and to sustain secondary-process thinking—all of which enabled him to remain in the analytic situation despite the anxieties engendered by a highly ambivalent transference neurosis.

1Presented at the Annual Meeting, Philadelphia, April, 1959. Chairman: Elizabeth R. Zetzel, M.D.

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Sleep, Time, and the Early Ego—Comments on the Development of the 24-Hour Sleep-Wakefulness Patterns as a Precursor of Ego Functioning11

Sanford Gifford M.D.

Sanford Gifford

Sanford Gifford  

SUMMARY

1. The adaptation of sleep and wakefulness to the 24-hour periodicity of night and day seems to reflect early ego development between the third and the fourteenth week of life, before the differentiation of the self from the environment is complete. Both the ego and the sleep rhythm evolve from an undifferentiated phase, when manifest behavior, homeostatic regulations, and congenital ego tendencies are inseparable, through an interrelationship between the infant's innate patterns of neurophysiological maturation and the response of the environment to his biological needs. The development of the sleep rhythm is mediated through the infant's relationship with his mother, communicated by her periodic absences or variations in responsiveness, which in

turn are influenced by her own adaptation to the 24-hour cycle of everyday life.

2. The differentiation of the sleep-wakefulness pattern is interpreted as a precursor of ego functioning because instinctual needs are systematically modified according to the reality principle. In this early form of reality perception, experienced by the infant as a periodicity in maternal responsiveness, the outer world is represented as a function of time. A primitive sense of time also exists in the infant's capacity to postpone immediate gratification, differentiating present and future by an act of anticipation.

3. At the twelfth to fourteenth week there is an acceleration in the adaptation of the sleep rhythm to the daytime segment of the 24-hour cycle, with waking in relation to parental activities and sleeping through the night with only one feeding. This change in pattern coincides with maturation of the perceptual apparatus, the appearance of the smiling response, and the capacity for purposeful finger sucking, which are associated, respectively, with pleasurable awareness of the outer world, the beginnings of more mature object relations, and increased differentiation of the self as a primitive body ego.

4. These conclusions were derived from Kleitman's and Engelmann's continuous observations on nineteen normal infants from the third to the twenty-sixth week of life. Their statistical data were interpreted as indicating three developmental phases, approximately corresponding to hypothetical phases of ego development. Using the continuity of the sleep-wakefulness pattern as a background, attempts were made to correlate these phases with other developmental landmarks, observed independently by psychoanalysts, child psychologists, physiologists, and biochemists. It was suggested that similar correlations with data from other fields may contribute to the validation of psychoanalytic theory and enlarge the conceptual basis for psychophysiological research.

The Face-Breast Equation11

Renato Almansi M.D.

Renato Almansi

Renato Almansi  

It is the purpose of this paper to present a number of data, drawn chiefly from clinical observation, which have certain characteristics in common, and to discuss the possible relationship of these phenomena to early perceptual experiences and early phases of ego development.

In his paper, "The Primal Cavity" (14), Spitz postulated that, from a visual standpoint, the Isakower phenomenon and Lewin's dream screen do not represent the breast but rather "the visually perceived human face." This view is consistent with Spitz's observation that the nursing infant's gaze is constantly fixed on the mother's face. It is also borne out by his studies on the importance of the infant's recognition of the Gestalt of the human face first, and the later identification of the mother's face, in the development of object relations and in the early mental life of the child. I believe that this hypothesis, which might appear somewhat daring at first glance, can be clinically substantiated.

In a recent paper (3) I have described a hypnagogic phenomenon which occurred in a scoptophilic patient at a time when his wife was pregnant, his financial security threatened, and intense frustration and anger toward the mother figure had come to the fore. The visual components of this phenomenon appeared to result from the fusion of two percepts: the image of a face, which was identified as loving and inviting at times and as angry and forbidding at other times; and the image of the breast, which had probably been perceived at a later stage of development. In this patient, the latter image, i.e., the breast, screened the former almost completely; only through the patient's numerous associations

[[RunningHead]]

A Historical Survey on the Teaching of Psychoanalytic Technique11

Rudolf Ekstein Ph.D.

Rudolf Ekstein

Rudolf Ekstein  

The sixty or more years of psychoanalytic history can be roughly divided into two equal parts as we evaluate the significance of current discussions concerning the teaching of psychoanalytic technique. The second part of this period, the last thirty years, constitutes the time span of organized psychoanalytic training, a time in which increased interest in the methods of training developed. It is only during this time that we find written evidence concerning issues of training and a developing literature on the subject which reflects a lively intellectual and frequently emotional struggle about training techniques. In his Presidential address in 1952 Knight (20) stated that most of the issues during the stormy history of the psychoanalytic development contributing occasionally to organizational difficulties, the splits of old, and the foundation of new institutes, had to do with issues of training.

The first period of psychoanalysis, a very creative one, could be considered ahistorical as far as the study of training methods is concerned. Balint (3) and Szasz (35) similarly speak of a prehistoric period. There are no documents explicitly investigating issues of training.

Submitted June 26, 1959

1Read at the Annual Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association in Philadelphia, April 25, 1959, as part of the panel on "The Teaching of Psychoanalytic Technique."500

[[RunningHead]]

Theoretical and Clinical Aspects of Overt Male Homosexuality

Charles Socarides M.D.

Charles Socarides

Charles Socarides  

In his introduction, Herman M. Serota stated that overt male homosexuality would be examined primarily from the point of view of ego psychology. This constituted the first panel exclusively devoted to the subject although two previous panels of the American Psychoanalytic Association had considered perversions in general.2 The rising incidence of overt homosexuality in urbanized cultures adds some importance to our considerations. He then detailed the topics for discussion as follows: (1) a historical survey of the literature on overt male homosexuality; (2) the structure and meaning of the homosexual love object, characteristics of the homosexual ego, the clinical appearance of the pathology, and the meaning of the fantasies that are acted out in this entity; (3) the genetic psychic determinants for overt homosexuality; and (4) therapeutic problems in the psychoanalysis of overt male homosexuals. He recalled that Robert Waelder's paper on "The Therapeutic Scope of Psychoanalysis"3 had already touched on the question of criteria for analyzability of homosexuality, and might serve as an introduction to it.

A detailed historical survey of the psychoanalytic literature on overt male homosexuality was then given by George H. Wiedeman. The many and varied contributions summarized by Wiedeman could be profitably reviewed under seven different headings. Beginning with constitutional versus environmental factors, he recalled that the point of departure for any study of homosexuality and perversions in general was Freud's "There Essays on the Theory of Sexuality" published in 1905. Until then heredity had been considered the decisive etiological factor and an inborn predisposition was considered paramount in these individuals. Freud expressed quite clearly

[[RunningHead]]

Rivalry with the Product—A Specific Type of Inhibition in Creative Activity11

Nathaniel Ross M.D.

Nathaniel Ross

Nathaniel Ross  

A divorced woman in her early thirties had been engaged in a struggle during analysis to resume the creative writing which she had given up many years previously. Between seventeen and twenty-one, she had had many articles and short stories accepted for publication. At twenty-one, following the termination of an intense love affair with a writer of international repute, which had lasted two months, she had given up creative writing. During the five years of her marriage to a brilliant, prolific, and cultivated creative artist, she produced nothing. After her divorce from him she obtained a position in a field allied to writing. A research worker of recognized ability, she wrote nothing beyond the requirements of her assignments. Following two abortive experiences in psychotherapy, she came to analysis because of her alarm at increasingly uncontrolled emotional outbursts at the love with whom she had been involved for two years, H., a man considerably inferior to her in cultural background and intellectual attainments. During her outbursts she was a screaming, possessive, demanding infant, utterly without shame or dignity. While she had always considered her emotional intensity a mark of distinction, she had now begun to regard these episodes with alarm, self-reproach, and disgust.

Submitted September, 1959

[[RunningHead]]

Schreber's Father11

William Niederland M.D.

William Niederland

William Niederland  

Senatspräsident Daniel Paul Schreber, who has aptly been called "the most frequently quoted patient in psychiatry" (3), was the second son of Dr. Daniel Gottlieb Moritz Schreber (1808-1861). The latter, a prominent physician and orthopedist, was also a prolific writer, lecturer, educator, and inventor of some sort. Freud, in his classic study on the son's Memoirs of My Nervous Illness(2), speaks of Dr. Schreber's "great reputation as the founder of therapeutic gymnastics in Germany, " mentions in some detail his multifold activities in the field of physical culture, and also points to the wide circulation of one of his books, Ärztliche Zimmergymnastik (Medical Indoor Gymnastics) (12), which at the time of Freud's publication had passed through twenty-six editions.

Freud also calls attention to Dr. Schreber's "lasting influence upon his contemporaries, " a point which acquires additional meaning when we learn that, according to my latest information (1958) from Germany, there are today over two million members in the so-called Schreber Vereine, associations dedicated to the propagation of physical culture, calisthenics, gardening, fresh-air activities, and the like.

It is apparent, then, that the man who as God became the central figure in the son's delusional system, was a person of unusual character and propensities.

Submitted January 4, 1959

[[RunningHead]]

The Influence of Psychosomatic Research on the Psychoanalytic Process11

Lawrence Roose M.D.

Lawrence Roose

Lawrence Roose  

Psychoanalysis has been increasingly used as a research technique in the experimental investigation of the nature of psychosomatic illness. As these experiments become more complex, psychoanalytic procedure is subjected to an increasing variety of external influences. To the extent that deviation from classical technique is pursued, its effect on patient, analyst, and the efficacy of analytic techniques must be considered carefully.

This study is intended (1) to describe the course of a psychosomatic research project where an attempt was made to utilize psychoanalysis as the principal research tool; and (2) to detail the effect on analytic procedures of various parameters and contaminants which were part of the experiment proper.

THE RESEARCH PROJECT

The Asthma Syndrome

The research project focused on an investigation of the asthma syndrome. Both physiologic and analytic procedures were carried on concurrently to test the tentative hypothesis that asthma appears as a regressive substitute in anxiety-provoking situations.

Submitted December 31, 1958

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

An Examination of Nosology According to Psychoanalytic Concepts11

Nathaniel Ross M.D.

Nathaniel Ross

Nathaniel Ross  

It is worthy of note that, as this Panel discussion proceeded, there emerged similar trends of thought concerning its subject matter; these had been germinating independently in the minds not only of the panelists, but in all likelihood of analysts in various parts of the country. This furnished the justification for the formation of such a panel and uniquely reveals its timeliness.

With one exception, the participants expressed dissatisfaction with our current nosological pattern, while at the same time they evinced no disposition to discard it entirely. (The latter alternative is not without its protagonists.) For a long time it has been apparent that a classificatory scheme, based phenomonologically on symptom clusters, and dynamically on levels of psychosexual development, is not adequate. There was no inclination to underestimate the complexity of the task of revising and modifying traditional nosological concepts in accordance with the now-recognized intricate interplay between instinctual and ego developmental levels, but the necessity of doing so was repeatedly emphasized. As for a possible framework upon which to construct a revised nosology, Leo Rangell, Eugene Pumpian-Mindlin, and Elizabeth Zetzel proposed, as a beginning, an examination of the epigenetic scheme of Erik Erikson.

The subject matter of the panel falls naturally into two categories—general considerations and specific ones. In the papers on specific problems, by Zetzel, John Frosch and Ross, considerations of an ego-psychological nature occupied a central role.

In his "Introductory Remarks on Problems of Nosology from the Psychoanalytic Standpoint, " Leo Rangell expressed the general dissatisfaction with the ambiguities, inconsistencies, inaccuracies, and exceptions which beset the traditional scheme of nosology. Upon close inspection, syndromes and individual symptoms invariably turn out to cover wider fields than originally conceived.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Further Observations on Primary Modes of Perception—The Use of a Masking Technique for Subliminal Visual Stimulation

Stanley Friedman Ph.D. and Charles Fisher Ph.D.

Charles Fisher

Charles Fisher  

SUMMARY

1. This study had as its purpose the validational testing of an observation of Urbantschitsch; namely, that a visual stimulus rendered invisible by covering it with sheets of paper still registered through the paper upon the perceptual apparatus. Methodology was based upon the possible recovery of transformations of this stimulus through the images of a group of subjects. Experimental controls, an a priori system of quantification, statistical applications, and "blind" scorers were part of the experimental design. The results supported this hypothesis of Urbantschitsch to a significant degree.

2. A subsidiary hypothesis, that the use of an "ego-impairing" drug (LSD-25) in subhallucinatory doses would increase the degree of delivery of percepts into subsequent imagery, was also tested and confirmed. The research techniques for this corollary experiment were the same as for the main study and used part of its research sample.

3. Experimental images under LSD showed certain qualitative differences from those obtained in the main experiment. These differences were an increase in color, light, and inanimate movements, and a decrease in the importance of the shapes of the image objects. It was speculated that these findings indicate the presence of an archaic level of perception that might precede the developmental phase dominated by primary-process transformations. In this archaic stage, visual perception would be Gestalt-free and dominated by color, light, and movement sensations.

4. Note was taken of certain similarities between the Urbantschitsch phenomenon and certain parapsychological findings. A

review of the literature devoted to the psi-phenomenon led to the interpretation that some of the results claimed for parapsychology may be based, instead, upon subliminal registration.

5. Individual differences in degree of utilization of subliminal stimuli were noted. The need for studies of individual differences was stressed. The markedly enhanced delivery of the subliminal stimulus following ingestion of LSD was interpreted as indicating that regression of the ego's perceptual apparatus is related to such increased utilization.

[[RunningHead]]

One Aspect of the Structure of Music—A Study of Regressive Transformations of Musical Themes

Stanley Friedman Ph.D.

Stanley Friedman

Stanley Friedman  

SUMMARY

1. This study, based on Kris's concept of art as a "regression in the service of the ego, " on Fisher's experimentally derived list of primary-process distortions of visual percepts, and on Fisher's application

of these transformations to the art of Picasso, had as its purpose an investigation of the changes in musical themes in the course of the piece.

2. It was found that eight of the ten perceptual transformations listed by Fisher were applicable to the sphere of music. These eight transformations were found very frequently in serious music, especially in developmental sections and codas.

3. While using slightly different terminology, certain musicologists stated that these transformations greatly enhance the affective impact of the melodies.

4. It was hypothesized that although the frequent use of regressive transformations is insufficient to explain the musical creative process, it may be related to our judgments of musical greatness in a manner not yet understood.

5. It was speculated that a musical theme represents an affect which temporarily activates some unconscious conflict. In listening, we participate in the temporary regression offered by the primary-process transformations of the thematic material and achieve a temporary mastery over this conflict. This mastery is experienced as aesthetic pleasure by the ego.

[[RunningHead]]

The Psychophysiological Basis for Two Kinds of Instincts—Implications for Psychoanalytic Theory

John Lilly M.D.

John Lilly

John Lilly  

INTRODUCTION

In recent years the brain systems which underlie and produce the phenomena called instinct have been delineated. For the first time the systems, which when stimulated cause either various kinds of pleasure or various types of negative avoiding, painful or fearful reactions, have been discovered and studied in great detail. Work has been accomplished on rats (16), cats (23), (1), monkeys (1), (7), dolphins (8), and in humans (20), (21), which has far-reaching implications for the systematic development of the theory of instincts.

In this paper the levels of discourse must be very carefully delineated to avoid confusion. Experiments using rats, cats, and monkeys involve extremely simple nervous systems compared with those of the human and of the dolphin. The phenomena elicited are completely reproducible. The responses to stimulation of the rat brain are simple and amazingly repetitive kinds of behavioral patterns: the cat introduced slightly greater variability; and the monkey steps up the complexity considerably. However, it is not until one investigates a brain comparable in size with that of the human that the full range of human complexity, which is dealt with in psychoanalysis, is achieved. The phenomena of speech, of writing, of love and hate of mental symbols, etc.

Submitted January 27, 1960

1Communication Research Institute, St. Thomas, U. S. Virgin Islands; formerly, National Institute of Mental Health, Bethesda, Maryland.659

[[RunningHead]]

Beyond the Bounds of the Basic Rule—Some Recent Contributions to Applied Psychoanalysis11

Heinz Kohut M.D.

Heinz Kohut

Heinz Kohut  

I

In an essay written in 1930, the great German writer Hermann Hesse spoke strongly against the abuse of psychoanalytic insights by literary critics, and protested, with trenchant irony, against the attempt to explain the personality of an author by the psychoanalytic investigation of his works: "If a patient should say to his analyst, 'My dear sir, I don't have either the time or the inclination for all these sessions, but I will give you here a package containing my dreams, wishes and fantasies in so far as I have written them down, partly in verse; please take this material and decipher from it, if you please, whatever you need to know'—what a scornful response would such a naïve patient receive from the doctor!" (7).

The problem which Hesse points up so sharply has remained an important one from the early days of psychoanalysis to the present. Can there be psychoanalysis without the persevering study of transference and resistance in the consistent and relatively objective form of free associations, supported by the (at least initially present) cooperation of the analysand?

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On Scientific Creativity

Peter Giovacchini M.D.

Peter Giovacchini

Peter Giovacchini  

SUMMARY

I have had the opportunity of studying eight scientists who have been universally recognized as talented and creative, some of them having made fundamental contributions to their fields. An attempt has been made in this paper to investigate the creative process in terms of the ego operations that may facilitate the creative potential, rather than only in terms of specific id factors or neurotic dynamisms and conflict situations. Such an approach does not add to our knowledge of the sources of creativity which may contain many variables, the constitutional being prominent among them. These patients all showed at one time or other varying degrees of decompensation, which made it possible to

424

study the various levels of the personality. When their defenses were re-established the character structure seemed to be that of an obsessive compulsive. Decompensation occurred when the ego was unable to subject id impulses to binding by higher ego systems. In the decompensated state, the ego was overwhelmed by primary-process operations, and the patient was unable to work. However, when compulsive defenses were working well, secondary-process operations predominated, and they were logical and coherent, but crass and materialistic, and unable to think imaginatively or produce creatively.

The creative operations of the ego were seen to consist of a balance of primary and secondary process. This ego has the ability to bind the chaotic impulses emerging from the unconscious, fuse them with external reality, and refine and integrate the product. When this was achieved, a new segment was added to reality. During creative periods the higher ego systems received energy from the emerging drives which led to hypercathexis and greater functional capacity in dealing with the very same drives and in subjecting them to the laws of the secondary process. The role of the memory trace in thought processes and creativity is also discussed.

[[RunningHead]]

The Psychology of Imagination11

Heinz Kohut M.D.

Heinz Kohut

Heinz Kohut  

David Beres, who presented the first paper, pointed directly to the importance of the panel's subject matter, the psychology of imagination, by stating that "the products of imagination are the data of the psychoanalyst's work. The symbol, the fantasy, the thought, the dream, " he said, "are to the psychoanalyst what the tracing of the electrocardiograph is to the cardiologist or the microscopic specimen to the pathologist." Beres defined imagination as "the capacity to form a mental representation of an absent object" and delineated it from those similar mental functions which occur in higher animals who "… can apprehend and respond to configurations and spatial relationships … in a concrete … fashion … in relation to direct, immediate, sensory stimulation." He added the important fact that precursors of imagination, which resemble the direct responses observed in higher animals, occur also in man during early infancy and with organic brain disease. He stressed, however, that there is a continuum of mental processes: "… from the ideational processes of the human infant and of infrahuman species, capable of perception, memory, learning and motor response, to the human thought processes which include abstract concept formation, language, and imagery." Similarly, he postulated a continuum in the progressive series of the contents of thought "from image, to idea, to abstract concepts." With Glover he assumed that "at the most primitive level there is immediate discharge without thought … a presymbolic phase of mental functioning" and, therefore, he pointed up the distinction between the presymbolic phase of mentation on the one hand, and the continuum of primary and secondary processes on the other. Beres did, however, not neglect the difference between primary- and secondary-process mentation as it applies to the problem of imagination, and he found a parallel to this psychoanalytically based distinction in Coleridge's use of the terms "fancy" and "imagination.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

An Epigenetic Approach to the Psychoanalytic Theory of Instincts and Affects11

Louis Kaywin M.D.

Louis Kaywin

Louis Kaywin  

SUMMARY

In brief, an examination of the psychoanalytic concept of instincts (general theory) and its two subsidiary hypotheses of differentiated libidinal and aggressive instincts (special theory) shows certain inconsistencies in relation to reaction patterns in general, when viewed according to epigenetic principles. The chief difficulties lie in the fact that they deal mainly in terms of energy, and further require the concept of differentiation of energy.

Alternative hypotheses are proposed, in terms of structurofunctional reaction patterns, which are more in keeping with epigenetic principles, and which seem better to account for the phenomena involved, i.e., certain mechanisms of mental function. A re-evaluation of the theory of affects, as structuralized positive and negative affect representations, in association with the self-system, permits a reformulation of the concepts of narcissism and masochism, without postulating differentiated libidinal and aggressive energies. Structural problems relative to the self-system are examined, and the id concept is reappraised.

The formulations presented not only bridge the gaps between

654

psychoanalytic clinical theory and a general psychoanalytic psychology, but, equally important, serve as a rapprochement between these and general biologic theories.

Regression and Fixation—Considerations Concerning the Development of the Ego11

Phyllis Greenacre M.D.

Phyllis Greenacre

Phyllis Greenacre  

SUMMARY

This paper is in a sense autobiographical since it is a condensed review of material published in many older papers, considered against the background of general developments in psychoanalytic thought. The papers here reviewed, sometimes with minor revisions of interpretation, are some dealing with preoedipal disturbances, with certain aspects of psychopathy, and with perversions, especially fetishism. While the original papers appeared as circumscribed studies arising from work with special clinical problems, it later became apparent that there were interconnections, and certain themes were repeated with varying clarity throughout different papers. Chief among these, although not specifically named, was the manifold interrelation of regression and fixation. In severe preoedipal disturbances due to traumatization from constitutional imbalances of the organism or from acute or long-sustained impact on it of an unfavorable external condition, the force of fixation appeared to play a larger role. There then tends to be an increase in narcissism, distortions in the ego development with marked increase and prematurity of the castration complex and more or less interference with good object relationship. In the most severe cases, an impairment of the full resonance of the oedipal crises follows, since it is too greatly invaded by elements derived from unresolved problems of body narcissism. This is in contrast to the dominance of regression as the main neurotic force in the psychoneuroses, where the failure to meet the emotional demands of the well-developed oedipal relationship institutes a partial backward movement to earlier way stations, minor fixation points, which might not otherwise be clinically manifest.

Other related questions are raised, notably consideration of certain problems of libidinal phase development, of female perversions, and of the origin of primary masochism. These are indicated as areas especially needing further study.

Psychosomatic Diseases in Children and Adolescents11

Bertram Gosliner M.D.

Bertram Gosliner

Bertram Gosliner  

In her opening remarks, Pavenstedt suggested that psychoanalytic study of the developments of early childhood offers a most fruitful approach to furthering our knowledge of psychosomatic disorders. This view was shared by members of the panel and other discussants who centered their interest upon early ego development. Discussion revolved about the dominant theme of endogenous and exogenous factors that foster or hinder early ego development. Pathology in the neonate was seen by all as the crucial determining factor in the genesis of psychosomatic illness.

Regression which dominates the psychopathologic phenomena of all psychosomatic disorders was discussed from different vantage points, but always central to the discussion was an evaluation of the quality of defect in the neonate's physiopsychologic state. The neonate's ability to achieve somatic and psychologic homeostasis to his distorted first orientation to need-gratification experiences regardless of incomplete fulfillment of earliest libidinal levels, was a focus for all discussion. It is noteworthy that all contributors spoke of regression to a state of development before the oral stage—a preconflictual primary functional level. It was most interesting and refreshing that in a full day's discussion of psychosomatic diseases there was no mention of specificity of the current conflict as a determinant of the type of psychosomatic illness manifested. Instead, the immediate conflict was seen by all as merely a precipitant and not as playing a determining role as to the type of illness manifested. Irene M. Josselyn, for example, specified, and the others inferred, that any conflict exerting sufficient stress may precipitate regression, the psychosomatic manifestations of which are determined by the above-mentioned neonatal vicissitudes.

Josselyn's report of her study of cases of ulcerative colitis had as its goal the testing of the hypothesis that psychosomatic illness has its roots in the experiences of the first few months of life. The newborn is at a "primary functional level, " is not a psychologic organism, but reacts to stimulation in a total and massive way, and without psychic content. Disorders are of excitation and discharge.

Book Notices

ADOLESCENCE AND THE CONFLICT OF GENERATIONS. By Gerald H. J. Pearson. New York: W. W. Norton & Co., 1958, 186 pp., $3.95.

This book is addressed primarily to professional workers in fields concerned with adolescents, but the author expresses the hope that it will also be read by parents and adolescents themselves. Pearson attempts to explain the reactions of adults to adolescents as his primary goal. In doing so, his approach is psychoanalytic. Adult intolerance of adolescents is produced by the repudiation of the repressed. The struggle of the adolescent toward the establishment of his identity is subject to the forces of the upsurging sexual impulses, the process of growth, regressive tendencies, the resistance of the older generation and cultural patterns which deny the adolescent a secure position and set of values in the world in which he lives. Pearson believes that there is an alternation in the attitudes of successive generations which reflects the rebellions of adolescents toward their immediate forebears, but states that he does not know whether this contributes the "evolution" which he discerns in the social scene with regard to interhuman relationships. He concludes his book with advice to the protagonists of the conflict and to those who deal with them on various levels of professional contact.

ADVANCES IN PSYCHIATRY. RECENT DEVELOPMENTS IN INTERPERSONAL RELATIONS. Edited and with an Introduction by Mabel Blake Cohen. New York: W. W. Norton & Co., 1959, 314 pp., $4.95.

This book consists of a group of articles which originally appeared between 1940 and 1957 in Psychiatry. The general orientation is reflected in the Introduction by the Editor: i.e., the last two decades of the growth of new concepts and techniques in psychiatry have largely arisen from the influence on the social sciences with their emphasis on group behavior. "And the logical consequence—that study of such behavior would then provide information about drives, motivations, conflicts, and characteristics of … people [was] not a part of theoretical psychiatry" twenty years ago. The editor deplores "in the analytic field proper, … lack of conscious and clear-cut recognition of the usefulness of the social sciences to psychoanalysis.
